title: Cat Panting with Mouth Open While Resting: Emergency Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat panting while resting? Open-mouth breathing in cats is a critical emergency. Learn the causes and why you must see a vet immediately.
content_html:
TL;DR: Open-mouth breathing in cats is a life-threatening emergency indicating severe respiratory distress. Seek immediate veterinary care if your cat is panting or breathing with their mouth open.

What does it mean if my cat is breathing with its mouth open?

Open-mouth breathing, often appearing as panting similar to a dog, occurs when a cat breathes through their mouth rather than their nose. While dogs pant to regulate temperature, cats typically only breathe through their noses. If your cat is resting and still panting, it is a clear sign that their body is struggling to move enough oxygen into the bloodstream.

Is open-mouth breathing in cats a veterinary emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High. This is a life-threatening medical emergency.
  • Unlike dogs, cats do not pant to cool down under normal conditions, especially while resting.
  • This behavior often indicates severe respiratory distress caused by underlying conditions such as congestive heart failure, feline asthma, or fluid in the lungs (pulmonary edema).
  • You should transport your cat to the nearest emergency veterinary clinic immediately.

How does a photo or video help the veterinarian triage my cat?

  • If it is safe to do so without delaying your departure, capture a 10-second video of your cat's breathing to help the veterinary team.
  • A video allows the vet to see the specific rhythm and effort of the breathing (such as abdominal movement) as it appeared at home.
  • Because cats often hide symptoms or change their breathing patterns when stressed at the clinic, this visual evidence helps the team triage your cat more accurately upon arrival.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Compromised breathing in cats manifests as an increased respiratory rate and effort, progressing to postural changes where the cat sits crouched on all four limbs with the sternum slightly elevated. Labored, open-mouth breathing and changes in mucous membrane color (gray or blue, indicating cyanosis) are late signs indicating significant loss of pulmonary function and impending pulmonary arrest. It is paramount to minimize motion of the head, neck, and spine when moving the animal.

Chapter: Emergency, Respiratory

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1663)

faqs.question: Can cats pant from stress or heat?
faqs.answer: While cats may pant briefly during extreme heat or a very stressful car ride, they should never do so while resting in a comfortable environment. If they are calm and still panting, it is a medical crisis.

faqs.question: What are other signs of breathing trouble?
faqs.answer: Look for 'abdominal breathing' (the sides of the belly moving in and out deeply), a hunched posture with the neck extended, or blue-tinged gums and tongue.

faqs.question: What should I do while driving to the emergency vet?
faqs.answer: Keep the car cool and quiet. Minimize handling of the cat to reduce their oxygen demand and keep them in a secure carrier to prevent further exertion.
